text: The surgeon performs a right upper lobectomy with robotic assistance, using an assistant port placed subcostally through the diaphragm. The inferior pulmonary ligament is mobilized, and station nine or eight lymph nodes are removed near the pulmonary vein. The lung is retracted anteriorly to expose the bronchus and posterior mediastinum, where station seven or subcarinal lymph nodes are excised. The caudate instrument is used to remove lymph nodes between the upper and lower lobe bronchus, revealing the posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery and opening the fissure.

The surgeon retracts the lung posteriorly and isolates the superior pulmonary vein. The fissure is entered, revealing lymph nodes around the middle lobe artery. A vessel loop is placed through the fissure, and the robotic green stapler is used to divide the fissure between the upper and lower lobe, as well as between the upper and middle lobe.

The surgeon uses a robotic white stapler to dissect the posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery. The fissure between the upper and middle lobes is divided using a robotic green stapler, facilitating the opening of the fissure. The lung is retracted posteriorly, exposing the anterior mediastinum. The superior pulmonary vein is identified, and the surgeon avoids the middle lobe vein while maneuvering around the superior vein. The arteries are taken first to prevent vein engorgement, and the completed fissure allows visualization of the truncus anterior.

The surgeon uses a vessel loop to elevate the artery, allowing the robotic white stapler anvil tip to safely divide the truncus anterior. The artery and veins are now separated, and the lung is retracted anteriorly to expose the bronchus to the upper lobe. The robotic green stapler is then introduced to address the bronchus.

The surgeon clamps the bronchus and inflates the lungs to ensure normal inflation of the middle and lower lobes. The bronchus is then divided, completing the mediastinal lymphadenectomy and right upper lobectomy. Saline is used to fill the chest to check for air leaks, revealing an air leak in the fissure. Pro-Gel is applied to seal the leak. A twenty French chest tube is positioned anteriorly under robotic guidance. The specimen is placed in an anchor bag.

The surgeon uses a 15-millimeter anchor bag to remove the specimen through the assistant port, causing the diaphragm to enlarge. A two-inch subcostal incision is made, and the diaphragm is repaired using a V-lock 2-0 monofilament suture. The XI robot assists in visualizing and repairing the diaphragm tear, which is aligned with the direction of the fibers, completing the right upper lobectomy and diaphragm repair.
